title: The Spread That Ate the Economy
subtitle: How Canada’s Credit Architecture Redirected Capital from Business Formation to Housing

notes.methodology: This report uses the supplied CSV seed datasets only. Where the pack provides only a point-in-time proxy rather than a true time series, the UI calls that out explicitly.
notes.thesisGuardrail: Treat this as an evidence-backed framework about amplification and incentives, not a monocausal proof.

executiveSummary: Housing is not only an affordability issue; it is also a capital-allocation issue.
Canada has made levered residential real estate easier to finance, standardize, and scale than most operating businesses.
Lower discount rates let investors accept lower yields, which can compress cap rates and lift values even when rents do not rise proportionally.
Higher ownership values relative to rental income can push households toward renting while making housing more attractive as an investment vehicle.
Business formation and productivity outcomes likely have multiple causes, but credit architecture may amplify the tilt toward housing.
The evidence here is directional and framework-building; it should not be read as a single-cause explanation for Canada’s economic performance.

sourceRegistry.officialSeriesOrTable: Bank of Canada group: chartered_bank_interest; V80691335 = 5-year conventional mortgage, V80691311 = prime rate
sourceRegistry.status: Seed includes latest five weekly rows from source page; full history available via Valet API
sourceRegistry.notes: Posted rates are typical rates offered by six major chartered banks; monthly rates are last Wednesday of month.

sourceRegistry.officialSeriesOrTable: Bank of Canada groups: A4_RATES_MONTHLY, A4_RATES_MORTGAGES, A4_RATES_EXTENDED
sourceRegistry.status: Seed includes 2025-10 to 2026-02 values from source page; full history via Valet API
sourceRegistry.notes: Rates are volume-weighted averages. Business loans total excludes non-residential mortgages to the personal sector as of Jan 2019.

sourceRegistry.status: Seed includes 1973-2010 data from Bank of Canada historical PDF; extend with StatCan V122521 / Valet current weekly series
sourceRegistry.notes: Rates are most typical charged by major chartered banks on residential mortgages, last Wednesday of month.

sourceRegistry.status: Seed includes recent observations; full history via FRED CSV
sourceRegistry.notes: Freddie Mac changed PMMS methodology on Nov 17, 2022.

sourceRegistry.status: Seed includes recent annual values; full history via FRED CSV

sourceRegistry.officialSeriesOrTable: FR 2028D, KC Fed Small Business Lending Survey
sourceRegistry.source: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City
sourceRegistry.status: Seed includes Q4 2024-Q4 2025 chart data from source page; aggregate Excel available from KC Fed
sourceRegistry.notes: Small business loan defined as loan to firm with $5M or less annual gross revenue.
Private business sector; includes growth rate of active businesses, entry rate, exit rate.
